reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children prioritise: (1) fair access to quality education for their own child regardless of family background; (2) predictable, transparent admission processes that do not require expensive private tutoring; (3) school choice that is not solely determined by wealth; (4) preservation of local school networks so commuting is feasible for working families.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–14, across socio-economic backgrounds, seeking equitable access to selective tracks without preparatory-coaching arms races, and stability of local school networks.
reactions.public_good_frame: Reducing the coachability advantage of high-SES families in admission serves broad equity: talented low-SES pupils currently screened out would gain access; middle-class families retain selective options but on fairer terms; the system becomes less dependent on private tutoring investment, lowering the barrier for families who cannot afford it.
reactions.argument: S1 addresses a direct pain point for lower-SES parents: the current exam-prep industry creates a de facto wealth tax on selective-track access. Regional equity quotas, if they work, mean my child's ability is valued, not my family's coaching budget. However, I have three conditional concerns: (1) The SES indicator must not misclassify or stigmatise (if address-based, does it flag my neighbourhood unfairly?). (2) The redesigned exam must genuinely reduce coaching—if test prep simply shifts to new formats, I've gained nothing. (3) Non-state schools must be brought into the same rules; if they are exempt, affluent families simply migrate there, and I am left in a worse-off general school. The scenario acknowledges all three uncertainties but does not guarantee they will be resolved before implementation.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would move to strong support if: (a) a feasibility study demonstrates the SES indicator does not misclassify and does not carry a stigma penalty; (b) pilot data from the redesigned exam shows coachability is genuinely reduced relative to the current exam; (c) church and foundation maintainers are contractually bound to equivalent equity rules before S1 is live. I would move to oppose if: (a) the SES indicator is found to be inaccurate or produces false negatives for disadvantaged pupils; (b) non-state schools are exempted or non-comply and capture the freed-up demand; (c) informal selection (interviews, 'fit' assessments) replaces the formal exam, recreating the same sorting by stealth.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children value: (1) delaying high-stakes sorting until their child is mature enough to make informed choices; (2) preservation of local general schools as viable, well-resourced options so that not going to a selective track does not mean educational abandonment; (3) predictability and clear communication about the trajectory so they can plan schooling and housing decisions.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–14 across socio-economic backgrounds, particularly those whose children may not be selected at age 10/12, seeking to preserve non-selective schools as credible, funded alternatives and to delay early tracking.
reactions.public_good_frame: Gradual reduction of early sorting attenuates the inequality-amplifying effect of early tracking documented in the international literature. Delaying selection gives more children time to mature cognitively and socially before streaming, reducing misallocation of talent. Keeping ambitious pupils in general schools longer improves peer composition for lower-SES classmates. However, this only works if general schools are adequately funded and staffed so that staying in them is genuinely a good outcome, not a consolation.
reactions.argument: The cap mechanism is conceptually sound—fewer seats, later selection, better-informed choice at 14. But S2 carries a transition-cohort risk that the scenario itself flags: as caps tighten, the remaining selective seats become more contested, and better-informed higher-SES families will game the transition. My child (age 10 in 2025) could face a narrower, more SES-skewed selective cohort as caps bite, even though the final steady state is more equitable. More critically, this only works if general schools are genuinely upgraded. If advanced-programme funding is not delivered credibly, or if the funding is cut in a fiscal crisis, I have no confidence that my child will not be trapped in an underfunded, peer-depleted general school. The scenario does not guarantee either. I also need transparent communication about the cap trajectory and my school's position in it; without that, I cannot plan.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would move to strong support if: (a) advanced-programme funding for general schools is legislated as a multi-party pact with an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority reversal threshold) so that it cannot be cut unilaterally; (b) a published cap trajectory is provided to parents at least 5 years in advance so that housing/school-choice decisions can be made with confidence; (c) longitudinal tracking shows that displaced demand does not migrate to non-state selective schools, or if it does, non-state selective places are also capped. I would move to oppose if: (a) the advanced-programme funding is not delivered or is cut before the transition stabilises; (b) middle-class flight to non-state schools materialises and is not countered; (c) the cap is frozen or reversed by a successor government, leaving my cohort in an unstable, partially-reformed system.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children value: (1) keeping their child in a common peer group with diverse SES backgrounds until age 14, reducing inequality reinforcement; (2) avoiding early, potentially irreversible streaming that may misallocate talent or crush motivation; (3) school communities that are not fractured by early selection; (4) later, more-informed choice of track when their child's interests and abilities are clearer. However, they also value: (5) credible quality assurance in general schools so that comprehensive schooling is not a euphemism for lower ambition; (6) manageable class heterogeneity for teachers and learning; (7) stability and not having their child be a pilot cohort for an unproven reform.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–14, particularly lower-SES parents whose children would remain in the common track longer and benefit from peer composition, but also middle-class parents concerned about educational quality and teacher capacity.
reactions.public_good_frame: Postponing selection to age 14 is supported by strong evidence from Finland and Poland: it weakens the SES-outcome gradient, does not lower mean performance, and gives all children time to develop before irreversible streaming. Keeping ambitious pupils in common schools longer improves peer composition for disadvantaged classmates. However, this is the most disruptive scenario: it requires a nationwide teacher retraining, absorbs high-achieving pupils into general schools where teacher shortage is acute, and invites middle-class exit to non-state schools. If that exit is large, equity gains for the most disadvantaged are lost, because they are left in a now-avoided general track.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that directly confronts the structural cause of inequality: early sorting amplifies family-background effects. I want this, but only if two conditions hold. First, my child's teachers must be equipped to handle heterogeneous classes—the Finnish model works because Finnish teachers are trained, well-paid, and few in number. Hungary has a documented teacher shortage. The scenario acknowledges this but does not resolve it; retraining and retention cannot happen concurrently. Second, my child must not be a pilot cohort. The scenario proposes a 2-3 district pilot FIRST, which is sensible, but this means early cohorts (my child's cohort, age 10 now) are at risk if the pilot fails and the policy reverses. The scenario notes the Polish reversal precedent, and a multi-party pact is proposed to anchor it, but pacts break. I need more than a pact; I need an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority override, long-term budget lock) and a clear fallback (S2 phase-down) if the pact collapses. Third, the middle-class flight risk is real. If affluent families exit to non-state comprehensive schools, the general track becomes more SES-segregated, not less. The scenario's equity_impact acknowledges this and notes it harms the least-mobile disadvantaged pupils most. That is a distributional equity problem within the disadvantaged group that I, as a lower-SES parent, cannot ignore.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would move to strong support if: (a) a rigorous pilot in 2-3 districts runs for at least 4 years before national rollout, with published evaluation showing gains in both equity (SES gradient) and quality (mean performance, teacher satisfaction); (b) a multi-party legislative pact with supermajority-reversal and multi-year budget-lock mechanisms is signed and publicly endorsed before any Nkt. amendment; (c) a pre-reform teacher-supply and retraining audit shows that differentiation training can be delivered without degrading classroom size or introducing new shortages; (d) longitudinal school-composition tracking during the pilot shows that non-state school enrolment does not spike or, if it does, non-state schools are brought under equivalent comprehensive rules. I would move to oppose if: (a) the pilot is skipped or compressed to <2 years; (b) a pact is not achieved and S3 is mandated top-down; (c) teacher shortage worsens before retraining can be deployed, creating visible quality loss; (d) early pilot cohorts experience a quality dip before the model stabilises; (e) middle-class exit to non-state schools is documented and not countered by bringing those schools into the comprehensive model.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children across socio-economic backgrounds expect: (1) structural fairness in school assignment, not the permanent compensation of an unjust structure; (2) long-term political predictability and protection from budget cuts; (3) their child's talent to be recognised early if present, not masked by lower-SES identity; (4) that their school is not a 'compensatory' or 'disadvantaged' school permanently, but a normal, resourced, valued option.
reactions.interest: Lower-SES parents particularly, who recognise that targeted compensation for an unjust structure legitimises that structure and makes them perpetual dependents on discretionary funding. Also middle-class parents in general schools who worry that S4 perpetuates two-tier outcomes and prevents their talented child from accessing selective tracks.
reactions.public_good_frame: S4 claims to neutralise the equity effects of early sorting without structural change. This is appealing politically but misleading in equity terms. Compensation schemes mask inequality rather than eliminate it. If a child is sorted into a general track at age 10 by an unfair process, and then given extra resources, the damage of the sorting—misallocation of talent, motivation loss, peer composition—is not offset by later compensation. The scenario itself acknowledges that no country has demonstrated compensation at Hungary's selection age can offset sorting effects; it only masks them. Sustained compensation may legitimise early selection permanently, entrenching the very inequality it targets.
reactions.argument: S4 is a trap. It offers political ease in the short term—no structural disruption, no teacher retraining, no middle-class opposition—but at the cost of permanent inequity. Here is why I oppose it. First, compensation does not offset sorting; it masks it. If my child is misidentified as lower-ability at age 10 by a coachable exam and sorted to a general school, giving that school extra funding does not recover the years of peer depletion and lower-track expectations. The scenario concedes this: 'no country has demonstrated this at Hungary's selection age.' Second, budget vulnerability: the scenario itself flags that compensation lines are 'first cut in fiscal consolidation.' This is not theory—it is Hungarian institutional reality. After 2008, every targeted-funding scheme was cut. I cannot build my family's educational strategy on funding that will disappear in the next fiscal crisis. Third, legitimacy: if compensation is sustained, it legitimises the sorting structure. Politicians will say 'we have fairly compensated general schools; selective tracks are justified.' The structure becomes entrenched, and my child's only way out is the coachable exam, which favours wealthy families. Fourth, the teacher shortage undermines S4 as much as S3, but S4 has no structural reform to justify the transition costs. S4 asks me to accept sorting + underfunded compensation, and hope the compensation budget holds. That is not a bet I will take with my child's education.
reactions.condition_to_change: I would move to conditional support (not strong support, because of the inherent structural problem) only if: (a) a longitudinal evaluation of a TEIP-style intervention in Hungary at age 10/12 selection demonstrates that compensation genuinely offsets (not masks) sorting effects, with effect size comparable to S2 or S3; (b) the targeted-funding lines are legislated with supermajority-reversal and multi-year budget-lock mechanisms so that they cannot be cut in fiscal consolidation; (c) the funding formulae are transparent and tested against actual needs so that the least-visible disadvantaged pupils are not missed; (d) teacher-supply constraints are addressed by salary premia that actually compete with non-educational sectors, not token increases. Even with all four conditions, I would still prefer S1 (reform within structure) or S2 (phase-down) because S4 leaves the unjust structure in place. I would only support S4 if S1 and S2 are politically impossible and S3 cannot be piloted.
